description: This figure presents the equilibrium Fe–Al phase diagram (Fig. 1), with temperature in °C on the vertical axis and aluminum content given in both weight percent (top) and atom percent (bottom). The diagram maps the phase stability regions from pure iron to pure aluminum, including disordered α-Fe (ferrite), γ-Fe (austenite), liquid (L), and ordered intermetallics—FeAl (B2), Fe3Al (D03), Fe2Al5, FeAl2, and FeAl3—together with two-phase fields such as α + B2 and α + D03. Annotated critical temperatures include the magnetic transformation at 770°C, the α ↔ γ transition near 912°C, melting and invariant reactions at 1530°C, 1394°C, 1310°C, 1215°C, 1164°C, 1171°C, 1157°C, and the Al-rich eutectic at 652°C (99.1 wt.% Al). The accompanying text notes that aluminum forms a disordered solid solution in iron up to about 11 wt.% Al, and beyond this level brittle D03-based Fe3Al and B2-based FeAl intermetallics appear. Thus, the diagram defines the thermodynamic limits for ductile, low-density ferritic Fe–Al alloy design.

This figure presents a density-composition plot showing the density reduction of Fe-Al and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy systems as a function of aluminum content. The x-axis represents Al content in wt.% ranging from 0 to 12, while the y-axis shows density in g·cm⁻³ from 6.5 to 8.0. Two datasets are plotted: blue circles connected by a line represent the binary Fe-Al system, starting from approximately 7.8 g·cm⁻³ at 0% Al and decreasing nearly linearly to about 6.85 g·cm⁻³ at 10.5% Al; red triangles connected by a line represent the Fe-Mn-Al-C system containing 14 wt.% Mn, starting from approximately 7.1 g·cm⁻³ at 7% Al and decreasing to about 6.7 g·cm⁻³ at 11% Al. Both curves demonstrate that increasing Al content progressively lowers the alloy density. At comparable Al contents, the Fe-Mn-Al-C system exhibits lower density than the Fe-Al system, indicating that Mn provides an additional slight density reduction effect as noted in the caption. The figure supports the core conclusion that Al is highly effective in reducing steel density—approximately 8 wt.% Al causing roughly 10% density reduction—and that Fe-Mn-Al-C multicomponent systems offer greater lightweighting potential than binary Fe-Al alloys for automotive applications.

A horizontal bar chart compares the mean grain size of three Fe-Al ferritic low-density steels against a traditional Al-free interstitial-free (IF) steel. The x-axis is labeled 'Mean grain size (μm)' spanning 0 to 100. The four bars represent, from top to bottom: 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti at approximately 18 μm, 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti at approximately 90 μm, 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti at approximately 69 μm, and IF steel at approximately 20 μm. Low-density steels with ultralow carbon and sufficient stabilizer content (6.8 and 8.1 wt.% Al) exhibit dramatically coarser grains than the traditional IF steel. In contrast, the 9.7 wt.% Al steel with higher carbon and insufficient stabilizer shows a finer grain size comparable to or slightly below the IF baseline. This figure demonstrates that grain size in Fe-Al ferritic lightweight steels is strongly governed by carbon level and stabilizing element content, where ultralow C and adequate stabilizer promote significant grain coarsening relative to conventional ferritic grades.

This bar chart compares the bake hardening (BH) response of five Fe-Al ferritic steels (Alloys 1–5) after a paint baking treatment of 2% deformation plus 170°C for 20 min. For each alloy, white bars represent pre-BH yield strength (YS), pink bars represent post-BH YS, and a blue line with solid circles denotes the YS increment (ΔYS). The nominal compositions (wt.%) are: 1 = 7Al-0.002C-0.01Ti, 2 = 7Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, 3 = 8Al-0.003C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb, 4 = 7.5Al-0.004C-0.02Nb, and 5 = 6.5Al-0.005C-0.01Ti-0.01Nb. Pre-BH YS values range from approximately 340 to 420 MPa, while post-BH YS values rise to roughly 420–500 MPa. The ΔYS remains nearly constant across all five alloys at about 75–80 MPa, indicating a robust BH effect that is relatively insensitive to the specific Al, C, Ti, and Nb levels tested. Alloy 3 achieves the highest post-BH YS near 500 MPa. The figure supports the conclusion that properly designed high-Al ferritic steels can gain approximately 80 MPa in yield strength through bake hardening via dislocation locking by interstitial carbon.

Figure 5 presents equilibrium phase fractions calculated by JMatPro 6.1 software for a lean ferrite-based duplex lightweight steel of composition Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C (wt.%) as a function of temperature from 0 to 1500°C. Four distinct curves are plotted: ferrite (red), austenite (blue), carbides (magenta), and liquid (black). Ferrite constitutes approximately 95 wt.% at low temperatures, undergoes a step decrease to around 88 wt.% near 700°C as austenite nucleates, then increases again toward 100 wt.% before the alloy melts. Austenite emerges near 700°C, peaks at roughly 10 wt.%, and continuously decreases until fully dissolving near 1250°C. Carbides precipitate below approximately 650°C at levels near 2 wt.% before complete dissolution. Liquid appears above about 1450°C and rises sharply. The figure demonstrates the inverse intercritical-phase-field behavior characteristic of lean Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex steels: austenite content decreases while ferrite content increases with rising temperature, contrary to standard low-carbon steels. This thermodynamic evidence defines the duplex-processing window and supports alloy design for density-reduced automotive steels.

This figure presents a schematic illustration of the phase transformation sequence leading to κ-carbide formation from ferrite in lean Mn-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys during continuous cooling. The diagram traces microstructural evolution through several stages: starting from the parent ferrite (α) phase, followed by nucleation and growth of austenite (γ). Subsequently, a ring of κ-carbide forms at the α/γ interphase boundary. With further transformation, this carbide ring undergoes fragmentation and globulization into discrete κ particles encircling the austenite grain. The pathway then bifurcates into two possible outcomes labeled “One or both”: either total or partial eutectoid decomposition of austenite into ferrite plus κ-carbide (shown as cross-hatched ferrite), or development of a modulated structure via precipitation of κ-carbide within the austenite matrix (γ → γ + κ-carbide). The schematic supports the core metallurgical conclusion that, in low-Mn steels, κ-carbides initiate at ferrite/austenite interfaces and that incomplete eutectoid reaction yields a modulated austenitic structure containing fine κ-carbide precipitates.

This figure comprises two pseudobinary phase diagrams showing the alloying effect on the solvus temperature of κ-carbide in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels. Panel (a) plots temperature (500–900 °C) versus Al content (5–11 wt.%) for Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys, and panel (b) plots temperature versus Mn content (23–30 wt.%) for Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys. Both panels display three phase fields: single-phase austenite (γ), γ plus intergranular κ-phase (γ + κ*), and γ plus intergranular and intragranular κ-phase (γ + κ + κ*), where caption identifies κ as intragranular and κ* as intergranular κ-carbide. In panel (a), increasing Al from approximately 5.5 to 10 wt.% markedly raises the upper solvus boundary from about 735 °C to over 830 °C and shifts the lower γ + κ* / γ + κ + κ* boundary upward from roughly 545 °C to 740 °C. Conversely, panel (b) shows that raising Mn from about 23 to 30 wt.% slightly depresses the solvus, with the upper boundary decreasing from ~800 °C to ~765 °C and the lower boundary from ~690 °C to ~665 °C. These opposing trends indicate that Al strongly stabilizes κ-carbide and elevates its dissolution temperature, whereas Mn mildly lowers it, supporting alloy design for precipitation hardening in the 500–750 °C aging window.

Figure 8 presents transmission electron micrographs revealing the microstructure development associated with the Shear Induced Plasticity (SIP) effect in Fe-(26–28)Mn-(11–12)Al-(0.9–1.15)C (wt.%) triplex steels following room-temperature tensile deformation. Subfigure (a) is a TEM bright-field image (scale bar 2 μm) showing shear bands on {111} planes in the austenite matrix. Subfigure (b) is a TEM dark-field image (scale bar 500 nm) taken with the superlattice reflection (210) of the [001] fcc zone axis, displaying a regular arrangement of nanosized j-carbides (20–30 nm) coherent to the austenitic matrix. The complementary images link the uniform carbide distribution to the organized shear band morphology. The figure supports the central argument that the SIP mechanism in these steels results from highly stable austenite; the accompanying text reports that this stability is characterized by a high positive Gibbs energy for austenite-to-martensite transformation (1755 J mol⁻¹) and a stacking fault energy of 110 mJ m⁻².

This figure presents a strength–elongation 'banana diagram' comparing several classes of low-density steels, plotting ultimate tensile strength (MPa) on the x-axis against total elongation (%) on the y-axis. Colored ellipses delineate the property ranges of distinct alloy families: red for Fe-Al ferritic steels; blue for Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex (α+γ), precipitation-hardened (α+κ), and triplex (γ+α+κ) steels; and purple for Fe-Al intermetallics. Visually, the Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex and triplex steels occupy the upper-right region, achieving superior strength–ductility combinations compared to ferritic and intermetallic counterparts. The plot also labels density reductions of 7.5–12.7% for ferritic steels, 10–20% for Fe-Mn-Al-C grades, and >25% for intermetallics. As the caption and citation text state, austenite-bearing duplex and triplex steels outperform ferritic steels because multiple strengthening and ductility-enhancing mechanisms are operative and these grades possess higher Al and Mn alloying contents. Overall, the diagram demonstrates that multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C systems simultaneously achieve substantial density reduction and an exceptional strength–ductility balance, supporting their promise for automotive lightweighting applications.

The figure displays engineering stress-strain tensile curves for ferritic low-density steels, comparing three Fe-Al-C-Ti alloys with a conventional interstitial-free (IF) steel. The curves are labeled 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti (magenta), 8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti (blue), 6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti (red), and IF (black). The 9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti alloy exhibits pronounced discontinuous yielding, annotated explicitly in the figure, with an upper yield point near 560 MPa followed by a yield drop before strain hardening, and fractures at approximately 15% strain with an ultimate tensile strength near 620 MPa. The 8.1Al alloy displays nearly continuous yielding with an ultimate tensile strength near 530 MPa and elongation around 16%, whereas the 6.8Al alloy and the conventional IF steel show fully continuous yielding. The IF steel possesses the lowest strength but the highest ductility, failing near 48% strain at approximately 300 MPa, while the 6.8Al alloy reaches approximately 470 MPa with elongation exceeding 30%. The figure demonstrates that free carbon in the ferritic matrix causes discontinuous yielding in high-Al ferritic steels, which can lead to surface stretcher marks during sheet forming, while lower or stabilized carbon contents promote continuous yielding comparable to conventional IF steels.

The figure displays a mechanical performance plot showing the true stress–true strain curve and the corresponding strain hardening rate versus true strain for an MBIP steel with nominal composition Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C. The red curve tracks true stress, which increases monotonically from approximately 500 MPa at zero strain to roughly 1600 MPa at a true strain near 0.65. The blue curve shows the strain hardening rate, beginning at an exceptionally high value near 2500 MPa, dropping sharply to a local minimum around 1550–1600 MPa at very low strain (~0.05), then rising to a broad maximum near 1900 MPa at a true strain of ~0.45–0.5 before declining and intersecting the true stress curve near 0.65 strain. The sustained elevation of the strain hardening rate—remaining above ~1500 MPa over most of the deformation range—is highlighted as a main characteristic of this austenite-containing low-density steel, distinguishing it from ferritic steels and underpinning the superior strength–ductility synergy in the Fe-Mn-Al-C system.

Figure 12 presents a line graph illustrating the systematic reduction of Youngs modulus in low-density steels as a function of aluminum addition. The horizontal axis indicates aluminum content from 0 to 10 wt.%, and the vertical axis shows Youngs modulus ranging from 140 to 220 GPa. Two literature datasets are plotted: blue circular markers with a solid line represent values reported by Rana et al. [6, 20], declining continuously from approximately 205 GPa at 0 wt.% Al down to roughly 154 GPa at 10 wt.% Al; red triangular markers with a solid line represent data from Frommeyer et al. [18], covering the approximate composition range of 6.5–9.5 wt.% Al and decreasing from about 192 GPa to 170 GPa. Both curves demonstrate a consistent negative trend, confirming that increasing Al content progressively lowers the elastic stiffness of lightweight ferrous alloys. This figure substantiates the critical caveat that, although Al additions effectively reduce steel density, the accompanying decrease in Youngs modulus directly compromises material stiffness, posing a significant constraint for automotive structural applications.

Figure 13 presents a flow chart of typical thermomechanical processing routes for producing lightweight Fe-Al-(C), Fe-Al-Mn-C, and Fe-Mn-Al-C ferritic and multiphase sheet steels at laboratory scale. The left branch shows the hot-rolling sequence: ingot casting, reheating at 1250 °C, rough rolling, a second reheating at 1250 °C, hot rolling to a final thickness of 3.5 mm with a finishing rolling temperature (FRT) of 900 °C, followed by run-out table cooling and coiling. The right branch depicts the downstream cold/warm processing chain: pickling at 85 °C in HCl, cold rolling or warm rolling at 250 °C to a final thickness of 1 mm, continuous annealing at 850–900 °C, and final aging at 400–700 °C. The flow chart explicitly labels Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C alloys for the hot-rolling path and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys for the cold-rolling and annealing path. The accompanying text explains that processing parameters depend on alloy chemistry, with high cooling rates after hot rolling recommended to suppress brittle j-carbide precipitation during coiling, and that coiling temperatures vary by alloy type (e.g., ~650 °C for ultra-low-C Fe-Al-(C) versus ~400 °C or room temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C). It also notes that warm rolling at 250 °C is required for ferritic alloys containing more than 8 wt.% Al to avoid cracking, and that cold reductions up to 66.67 % can be applied to multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.

Figure 14 presents a CAE engineering simulation comparing shell thickness distributions across the body-in-white (BIW) of a Volkswagen Golf V, with (a) showing the baseline design using conventional HSLA steels and (b) showing the design using a ferrite-based duplex low-density steel (Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C). Subfigure (a) employs a color scale from 0.750 mm (white) to 3.000 mm (magenta), revealing that many structural members are colored green (1.875 mm) and yellow (2.250 mm). Subfigure (b) uses a scale from 0.600 mm to 4.000 mm, and a visible shift toward thinner gauges is apparent, with an increased prevalence of dark blue (1.167 mm) and cyan (1.733 mm) regions relative to the HSLA baseline. The caption states that this reduction in shell thickness, required because the low-density steel has a lower Young's modulus yet must maintain the same torsional stiffness, was predicted to achieve a 14.5% weight saving in the BIW. These comparative thickness maps support the core conclusion that ferrite-based duplex low-density steels can replace HSLA grades in automotive BIW structures without compromising stiffness performance.
